Jeremiah.lastChapterSummary: Previously: The Lord declares war on Babylon—the city that enslaved His people crumbles. Now the temple burns.

Revelation.storyArcSoFar: ...own nations with a sword from his mouth. The beast and false prophet are thrown into the lake of fire. Chapter 21: Previously: The beast and false prophet are cast into the lake of fire. Satan is bound for a thousand years, then released for one final rebellion—crushed by fire from heaven. Chapter 22: Previously: Death is defeated, Satan cast into the lake of fire. The old heaven and earth pass away—"Behold, I am making all things new!" Now a river of life flows from God's throne, crystal-clear.
Revelation.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Death is defeated, Satan cast into the lake of fire. The old heaven and earth pass away—"Behold, I am making all things new!" Now a river of life flows from God's throne, crystal-clear.

Lamentations.storyArcSoFar: ...ess." Yet hope breaks through: "His mercies are new every morning." Now Jerusalem's gold has lost its luster. Princes once robust as rubies now crawl the streets, skin blackened, unrecognizable. Chapter 5: Previously: Jerusalem's gold has turned to dust, princes crawl unrecognized through streets littered with their dead. Now the survivors cry out: "Remember, LORD, what has happened to us!" Like a city under siege, they inventory their losses—inheritance gone, mothers defiled, children enslaved.
Lamentations.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Jerusalem's gold has turned to dust, princes crawl unrecognized through streets littered with their dead. Now the survivors cry out: "Remember, LORD, what has happened to us!" Like a city under siege, they inventory their losses—inheritance gone, mothers defiled, children enslaved.

Ezekiel.storyArcSoFar: ...ter 46: Previously: God divides the land itself—holy portions for priests, Levites, and prince. Now God establishes the rhythm of worship—sabbaths, new moons, sacred feasts. Chapter 47: Previously: God establishes the rhythm of worship—sabbaths, new moons, sacred feasts. Now the man leads Ezekiel to the temple's threshold. Chapter 48: Previously: Water flows from the temple, bringing life wherever it goes—trees bear fruit year-round, leaves heal nations. Now God divides the promised land itself.
Ezekiel.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Water flows from the temple, bringing life wherever it goes—trees bear fruit year-round, leaves heal nations. Now God divides the promised land itself.

2 Corinthians.storyArcSoFar: ...les—"Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light." He lists his sufferings: beatings, shipwrecks, sleepless nights. Now he shifts to glory: "I know a man caught up to the third heaven." Paradise visions flood—yet God gives him a thorn in the flesh. Chapter 13: Previously: Paul boasts in weakness—"My grace is sufficient for you." His thorn in the flesh remains, proving God's power perfects through suffering. Now Paul warns: "This is the third time I am coming to you." Every sin will be judged.
2 Corinthians.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Paul boasts in weakness—"My grace is sufficient for you." His thorn in the flesh remains, proving God's power perfects through suffering. Now Paul warns: "This is the third time I am coming to you." Every sin will be judged.

Hosea.storyArcSoFar: ...n, Israel, to your God." Yet Jacob's descendants still grip deceit like their father once gripped his brother's heel. Chapter 13: Previously: Jacob's descendants still chase the wind, gripping deceit like their father gripped Esau's heel. Now God's voice trembles with memory: "When Ephraim spoke, men trembled—he was exalted in Israel. Chapter 14: Previously: Ephraim spoke and nations trembled—but Baal-worship brought death. God remembers nurturing them like a child, yet they sacrificed to idols.
Hosea.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Ephraim spoke and nations trembled—but Baal-worship brought death. God remembers nurturing them like a child, yet they sacrificed to idols.

Joel.storyArcSoFar: ... locust plague as a window into God's coming judgment and future restoration. Chapter 2: Previously: A locust army devours everything—Joel calls the people to weep and pray. Now the prophet cries: "Blow the trumpet in Zion!" A darker swarm approaches—the day of the Lord itself, an army that makes the earth tremble. Chapter 3: Previously: God promises to restore Judah and pour out His Spirit on all people—sons and daughters will prophesy. Now Joel summons the nations to the Valley of Jehoshaphat.
Joel.lastChapterSummary: Previously: God promises to restore Judah and pour out His Spirit on all people—sons and daughters will prophesy. Now Joel summons the nations to the Valley of Jehoshaphat.

Amos.storyArcSoFar: ...ugh judgment, but Israel's elite lounge in luxury as the nation crumbles. Now God shows Amos three visions of coming destruction—locusts, fire, a plumb line. Chapter 8: Previously: God shows Amos three visions of coming destruction—locusts, fire, a plumb line. Now Amos sees a basket of ripe fruit. Chapter 9: Previously: Amos sees a basket of ripe fruit—Israel's time is up. Now God stands at the altar, commanding total destruction: "Strike the tops of the pillars!" The earth quakes, sinners fall.
Amos.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Amos sees a basket of ripe fruit—Israel's time is up. Now God stands at the altar, commanding total destruction: "Strike the tops of the pillars!" The earth quakes, sinners fall.

1 Peter.storyArcSoFar: ...er well—silent like Christ before accusers, trusting God's justice. Now he pushes harder: "Since Christ suffered in his body, arm yourselves with the same attitude." The old life is finished—no more living for evil desires. Chapter 5: Previously: Peter taught believers to arm themselves with Christ's attitude—suffering in the body ends sin's power. Now Peter speaks as fellow elder: "Shepherd God's flock willingly, not for dishonest gain." The chief Shepherd is coming with glory that never fades.
1 Peter.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Peter taught believers to arm themselves with Christ's attitude—suffering in the body ends sin's power. Now Peter speaks as fellow elder: "Shepherd God's flock willingly, not for dishonest gain." The chief Shepherd is coming with glory that never fades.

Obadiah.storyArcSoFar: Chapter 1: The prophet Obadiah wrote this vision around 586 BC, shortly after Jerusalem's fall. This is the Bible's shortest book—a fiery prophecy against Edom for betraying Judah in her darkest hour.
Obadiah.lastChapterSummary: The prophet Obadiah wrote this vision around 586 BC, shortly after Jerusalem's fall. This is the Bible's shortest book—a fiery prophecy against Edom for betraying Judah in her darkest hour.

Jonah.storyArcSoFar: ...ss compassion for all nations, even Israel's enemies. Chapter 2: Previously: Jonah flees God's call to Nineveh, boards a ship, and is hurled into the sea during a violent storm. Now from the belly of a great fish, Jonah cries out to the Lord. Chapter 3: Previously: Jonah prays from the fish's belly, crying out to God who hears him. The Lord commands the fish—it vomits Jonah onto dry land. Chapter 4: Previously: Jonah obeys—finally. He walks into Nineveh proclaiming its destruction in forty days.
Jonah.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Jonah obeys—finally. He walks into Nineveh proclaiming its destruction in forty days.

Micah.storyArcSoFar: ...aching earth's ends. Chapter 6: Previously: From Bethlehem comes a ruler whose origins are ancient—a shepherd-king bringing peace to earth's ends. Now God hauls Israel into court: "What have I done to weary you? Chapter 7: Previously: God hauls Israel into court—"What have I done to weary you?" He demands justice, not empty rituals. Now Micah surveys the ruins: "Do not trust a neighbor; put no confidence in a friend." Faithful people have vanished from the land, everyone hunts their own brother.
Micah.lastChapterSummary: Previously: God hauls Israel into court—"What have I done to weary you?" He demands justice, not empty rituals. Now Micah surveys the ruins: "Do not trust a neighbor; put no confidence in a friend." Faithful people have vanished from the land, everyone hunts their own brother.

Nahum.storyArcSoFar: Chapter 1: Nahum prophesied around 663-612 BC, between Nineveh's peak and its fall. This book pronounces God's righteous judgment on Assyria's brutal capital. Chapter 2: Previously: God declares He is coming to judge Nineveh—the jealous Lord who repays His foes. Now the destroyer advances on the city. Chapter 3: Previously: God's destroyer advances on Nineveh, His righteous judgment unfolding. Now the Lord exposes the city's wickedness: "Woe to the city of blood, full of lies, full of plunder!" Bodies pile in the streets as Nineveh's enslaved nations watch.
Nahum.lastChapterSummary: Previously: God's destroyer advances on Nineveh, His righteous judgment unfolding. Now the Lord exposes the city's wickedness: "Woe to the city of blood, full of lies, full of plunder!" Bodies pile in the streets as Nineveh's enslaved nations watch.

Habakkuk.storyArcSoFar: Chapter 1: The prophet Habakkuk wrote this book around 605 BC, as Babylon rose to power. Habakkuk wrestles with God's justice—why does evil seem to triumph? Chapter 2: Previously: Habakkuk cries out—how long must wickedness prevail? God's answer shocks him: "I'm raising up the Babylonians." Even more unjust! Chapter 3: Previously: God declares judgment—arrogant Babylon will fall, but the righteous will live by faith. Now Habakkuk's prayer erupts in fiery poetry.
Habakkuk.lastChapterSummary: Previously: God declares judgment—arrogant Babylon will fall, but the righteous will live by faith. Now Habakkuk's prayer erupts in fiery poetry.

2 Peter.storyArcSoFar: ...usly: Peter urged believers to grow in godliness, escaping corruption through Christ's divine power. Now false teachers creep in like prowlers, promising freedom while peddling bondage. Chapter 3: Previously: Peter warns of false teachers who deny Christ and lead others astray, promising freedom while enslaved to corruption. Now scoffers emerge, mocking the promise of Christ's return: "Where is this coming he promised?" But Peter reminds them—the Lord's apparent delay is mercy, not indifference.
2 Peter.lastChapterSummary: Previously: Peter warns of false teachers who deny Christ and lead others astray, promising freedom while enslaved to corruption. Now scoffers emerge, mocking the promise of Christ's return: "Where is this coming he promised?" But Peter reminds them—the Lord's apparent delay is mercy, not indifference.

Zephaniah.storyArcSoFar: ... for the faithful remnant. Chapter 2: Previously: God announces cataclysmic judgment—earth swept clean, a reversal worse than Noah's flood. Now Zephaniah pleads: "Seek the LORD, all you humble of the land." He calls the meek to righteousness before the day of wrath arrives. Chapter 3: Previously: After announcing earth-sweeping judgment, Zephaniah pleads for the humble to seek righteousness before wrath arrives. Now Jerusalem stands rebellious, officials roaring like lions, prophets treacherous.
Zephaniah.lastChapterSummary: Previously: After announcing earth-sweeping judgment, Zephaniah pleads for the humble to seek righteousness before wrath arrives. Now Jerusalem stands rebellious, officials roaring like lions, prophets treacherous.

Haggai.storyArcSoFar: Chapter 1: The prophet Haggai preached in 520 BC during Jerusalem's restoration after Babylonian exile. This book calls God's people to rebuild the temple they abandoned for their own comfort. Chapter 2: Previously: God's people abandon the temple for their own homes—until Haggai stirs them to rebuild. Now, a month later, discouragement sets in.
Haggai.lastChapterSummary: Previously: God's people abandon the temple for their own homes—until Haggai stirs them to rebuild. Now, a month later, discouragement sets in.

Zechariah.storyArcSoFar: ...ly: The shepherd breaks his staff called Favor, then Union—Israel's covenant shattered for thirty pieces of silver. Now Jerusalem becomes an immovable rock. Chapter 13: Previously: Jerusalem becomes an immovable rock, nations gather against her. Now God promises to pour out "a spirit of grace and supplication"—they will look on the one they pierced and mourn bitterly. Chapter 14: Previously: God promises to pour out a spirit of grace—they will mourn for the one they pierced. Now the day arrives.
Zechariah.lastChapterSummary: Previously: God promises to pour out a spirit of grace—they will mourn for the one they pierced. Now the day arrives.

James.storyArcSoFar: ...rests ablaze. Chapter 4: Previously: James warns about the tongue—a tiny spark that sets forests ablaze. Now he asks: "Where do wars and fights among you come from?" The answer cuts deep: "Your desires battle within you." They want but don't have, so they kill and covet. Chapter 5: Previously: James asks where wars come from—your desires battle within you, wanting what you can't have. Now he turns to the rich: "Your wealth has rotted, your gold corroded." Their stolen wages cry out against them.
James.lastChapterSummary: Previously: James asks where wars come from—your desires battle within you, wanting what you can't have. Now he turns to the rich: "Your wealth has rotted, your gold corroded." Their stolen wages cry out against them.

Now the messenger arrives: "But who can endure the day of his coming?" God promises a refiner's fire to purify the sons of Levi. Chapter 4: Previously: God promises a refiner's fire to purify the sons of Levi—but who can endure his coming? Now the final prophecy blazes: "For behold, the day is coming, burning like an oven." The arrogant will be stubble.
Malachi.lastChapterSummary: Previously: God promises a refiner's fire to purify the sons of Levi—but who can endure his coming? Now the final prophecy blazes: "For behold, the day is coming, burning like an oven." The arrogant will be stubble.
